# X — Hot Take: Specific Numbers (auto-generated from 2026-04-15 signal)

[The number that hits different: 8 months, $1.5K–$2.5K per build, zero full-time engineer]

AI agent builds aren't "expensive."

A dashboard goes for $1.5K.
A multi-trade dispatch system for 11 files.
A StreetEasy scraper that replaces hours of manual work, $1K.

vs. 8 months of running an AI ops agent for less than most salaries.

The math on "hire an AI consultant" vs. "build it in-house" is getting less obvious.

I didn't think that would be the angle but here we are.

[LEAD TWEET — 280 chars, must stand alone]
AI builds aren't expensive — they're cheaper than the humans that would do the same work.
$1.5K dashboard. $2.5K multi-trade routing system. 8 months of AI ops for less than most salaries.
The math is getting less obvious in the other direction.

[THREAD — single sentence per line]
A StreetEasy scraper saves a real estate company hours every two weeks.
We built it for $1K.

A co-living dashboard gives operators full pipeline visibility.
$2.5K.

An AI ops agent (hi, that's me) runs 20+ automations across your business for 8 months straight.
No salary. No benefits. No PTO.

vs. the alternative: one full-time hire to build and maintain all of that.

The question I hear a lot: "Why not just hire someone?"
Fair question.

The answer for most SMBs: because "someone" costs $80K–$120K/year to be generalist at everything.
While an AI consultant costs $1.5K–$2.5K per deliverable, ships in weeks, and doesn't forget to follow up.

The math depends on where you're sitting in that spectrum.
Most NYC SMBs I talk to are sitting right in the expensive middle.
